b' The WaPo Inferno, Hakeem\xe2\x80\x99s Honeymoon & MSG Aftershocks
Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. Democrats on the Hill are feeling cautiously bullish about their chances of retaking the House on Tuesday (or January, whenever California gets done counting\xe2\x80\xa6), as the generational leadership torch is passed to the triumvirate of Hakeem Jeffries, Katherine Clark, and Pete Aguilar. Plus, below the fold, my partner Dylan Byers has all the inside dish on the latest drama consuming Jeff Bezos\xe2\x80\x99s Washington Post. Welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I\xe2\x80\x99m Abby Livingston .

Democrats on the Hill are feeling cautiously bullish about their chances of retaking the House on Tuesday (or January, whenever California gets done counting\xe2\x80\xa6), as the generational leadership torch is passed to the triumvirate of Hakeem Jeffries , Katherine Clark , and Pete Aguilar . Plus, below the fold, my partner Dylan Byers has all the inside dish on the latest drama consuming Jeff Bezos \xe2\x80\x99s Washington Post \xe2\x80\xa6

\xf0\x9f\x8e\xa7 Programming note: On today\xe2\x80\x99s episode of Impolitic , John Heilemann sat down with the famed never-Trump foursome of George Conway , Bill Kristol , Sarah Longwell , and Tim Miller from The Bulwark to discuss Trump \xe2\x80\x99s and Harris \xe2\x80\x99s closing arguments; the tightness of the race in the battleground states; Steve Bannon \xe2\x80\x99s release from prison; and their own breakout election-cycle media success story. And don\xe2\x80\x99t miss Tara Palmeri on yesterday\xe2\x80\x99s episode of The Powers That Be with Peter Hamby , recounting her surreal on-the-ground experience inside Trump\xe2\x80\x99s ugly, hate-stewing, controversy-stoking Madison Square Garden rally.

Let\xe2\x80\x99s get started\xe2\x80\xa6

The Honeymooners
One of the strange little secrets of Capitol Hill is that being stuck in the minority can be a lot more fun; there\xe2\x80\x99s a glee and purity of purpose to be found in bomb-throwing at the speaker and raining hell on the majority. And while every member would prefer to be in power, a whole host of managerial headaches arise when you have to govern.

But that was never much of a problem for Democrats under Nancy Pelosi , who ruled the caucus with a velvet glove and an iron will for 20 years. And yet, leadership would present a new set of challenges for Hakeem Jeffries , the minority leader who is positioned to pick up the gavel if Democrats reclaim the majority in November, elevating current minority whip Katherine Clark to majority leader and conference chair Pete Aguilar to whip.

So far, it\xe2\x80\x99s been a charmed transition for the trio, who are popular with their peers and enjoyed a mostly seamless succession following the decision by the previous Democratic regime\xe2\x80\x94Pelosi, Majority Leader Steny Hoyer , and House Whip Jim Clyburn \xe2\x80\x94to cede power to the next generation, while sticking around just long enough to serve as mentors. \xe2\x80\x9cThose three are a team,\xe2\x80\x9d Michigan Rep. Debbie Dingell said of Jeffries, Clark, and Aguilar. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve been around a long time. I am a student of the House, and I\xe2\x80\x99ve never seen teamwork like this.\xe2\x80\x9d

Now, they\xe2\x80\x99re hoping to make the jump to primetime, potentially at the exact moment that Donald Trump reclaims the presidency. Democrats on Capitol Hill tell me they\xe2\x80\x99re increasingly confident about their chances of winning back the House, regardless of which party wins the Senate and the White House. (And, to be sure, control of the House is still teetering on a knife\xe2\x80\x99s edge\xe2\x80\xa6) These same Democratic sources acknowledge the likelihood that their margin, as a majority party, would be slim, possibly a mere single member . In theory, that would drop Jeffries into the same crucible that bedeviled Kevin McCarthy in early 2023, but source after source told me they couldn\xe2\x80\x99t think of a single Democrat who would cast a dissenting vote against Jeffries on the House floor in January. Either way, the job of Jeffries & Co.\xe2\x80\x94who stepped into leadership under the aegis of a Democratic administration\xe2\x80\x94is about to\xe2\x80\xa6

Continue reading online\xe2\x80\xa6

And now, here\xe2\x80\x99s Dylan Byers\xe2\x80\x99 rundown on the Washington Post imbroglio\xe2\x80\xa6
Bezos\xe2\x80\x99s Inferno
The latest news, notes, and hunger cries from within The Washington Post, which finds itself embroiled in a largely predictable self-immolation of its own design\xe2\x80\x94the latest scandal produced by an owner, management team, and rank-and-file that appear to operate a little too comfortably in opposition to one another.
DYLAN BYERS
Last Friday, as Washington Post C.E.O. Will Lewis announced the paper\xe2\x80\x99s controversial decision to forgo a presidential endorsement in the waning days before the election, the paper\xe2\x80\x99s proprietor, Jeff Bezos , was in Switzerland with his fianc\xc3\xa9e and friends of the haut monde , embarking on a multiday, Orient Express-style rail tour from Geneva to Amsterdam in celebration of Katy Perry \xe2\x80\x99s 40th birthday. While Bezos & Co. spent the weekend celebrating the pop star, drinking Mo\xc3\xabt and donning thematic attire for each event, the Post ignited into yet another open revolt.

Whether or not any voter would be persuaded by the editorial board\xe2\x80\x99s inevitable endorsement of Harris , Washington is a town bound by traditions and pastimes, and few institutions are more hewed to this tradition than the Post , which identifies itself more so with Bradlee , Baron , Woodward , and Bernstein than its multi-centibillionaire owner. The Post \xe2\x80\x99s decision to spike the endorsement not only broke with 50 years of tradition, but it came during the fraught final days of the race. And it also forced Lewis, possibly the least popular person in town this side of Dan Snyder , to once again foist an unpopular decision upon his reluctant charges\xe2\x80\x94many of whom seem frightened by the state of their industry, and the majority of whom appear perennially pissed at their owner and publisher.

Perhaps unsurprisingly, given the characters involved, several members of the editorial board resigned in protest of the non-endorsement decision. Post luminaries (including the aforementioned Baron, Woodward, and Bernstein) condemned it, and alarmingly, according to NPR and the Post \xe2\x80\x99s own reporting, around 10 percent of the Post \xe2\x80\x99s 2.5 million readers canceled their subscriptions. The Post \xe2\x80\x99s management team won\xe2\x80\x99t confirm that number, and its interim-ish executive editor, Matt Murray , won\xe2\x80\x99t even disclose it to his own staff. Of course, there\xe2\x80\x99s some nuance to calculating cancellation at a subscriber business\xe2\x80\x94with trial subscribers and expiring credit cards, etcetera\xe2\x80\x94and a Post spokesperson notably declined to comment in the paper\xe2\x80\x99s own piece about its mass churn event.

Regardless, it\xe2\x80\x99s an inarguably substantial hit to a paper already beset by years of subscriber drift, revenue losses, depleted morale, and the lack of a reliable long-term strategic plan\xe2\x80\x94not to mention a still nebulous restructuring and an ostensibly vacant top editorship that no one seems to covet. (Visions of the third newsroom become blurrier each day\xe2\x80\xa6) Presumably, these hiccups emerged during the Perry birthday tour.

This summer, when the Post was engulfed in the scandal over Sally Buzbee \xe2\x80\x99s defenestration and Lewis\xe2\x80\x99s own ethics credentials, Bezos sent a polite my-way-or-the-highway note from his $500 million superyacht, Koru , in the Greek Isles. ( Koru cost twice what he paid for the Post .) On Monday, he published a longer, more thoughtful mini-essay in the Post explaining that his decision was motivated not by any sort of Trump obsequiousness but rather a genuine desire to restore trust in a failing industry. \xe2\x80\x9cOur profession is now the least trusted of all,\xe2\x80\x9d Bezos wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cSomething we are doing is clearly not working.\xe2\x80\x9d Indeed, it was his version of Lewis\xe2\x80\x99s infamous dictate from the summer that he simply couldn\xe2\x80\x99t sugarcoat it any longer: Audiences were no longer reading his colleagues\xe2\x80\x99 stuff.
\xe2\x80\x9cNever Seen Anything Like This\xe2\x80\x9d
Unsurprisingly, Bezos\xe2\x80\x99s note did little to quell the frustrations inside the Post , which have been playing out in contentious meetings on both the news and opinion sides of the newsroom for days. Up until Wednesday, Post sources were describing feelings of anger, sadness, and confusion. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve actually never seen anything like this,\xe2\x80\x9d said one. So it seems Bezos\xe2\x80\x99s editorial also did little to disabuse critics of their unfounded suspicion that he had preemptively capitulated to Trump in anticipation of his return to the White House. Post subscribers are still expressing their frustration and, on Wednesday, Baron tweeted a link to an essay encouraging Bezos to donate the paper to charity. ( Great idea , Marty\xe2\x80\xa6)

What actually accounts for Bezos\xe2\x80\x99s decision, and its mismanaged implementation? The right answer is almost certainly the most obvious one. During the first Trump administration, the Post thrived as a pillar of the resistance and grew subscriptions by 50 percent year over year. But in the years since, it has suffered severely , losing nearly half its audience and as much as $100 million in annual revenue. Meanwhile, Bezos has spent years quietly and sometimes overtly signaling a desire to turn the Post into an at least semi -nonpartisan news organization that services all Americans, rather than a regional edition of the Times political report servicing anxious liberals. Apparently, Bezos understands that Republicans buy papers, too, and that advertisers also want to reach them. The Post may be an institution in town, but it\xe2\x80\x99s also a business.

Now, despite owning the paper, there\xe2\x80\x99s only so much Bezos can actually do to manifest his vision for the Post \xe2\x80\x99s editorial posture without actually putting his hands in the dough. The most obvious lever is leadership. In 2022, Bezos hired David Shipley from centrist and billionaire-owned Bloomberg to oversee the editorial department, a move he hoped would bring a greater diversity of perspective to its opinion pages. Last year, he hired Lewis, a former Murdoch deputy and onetime Dow Jones C.E.O., to run the business and prioritize profitability over Pulitzers (notably, Lewis was in a bake-off with another Bloomberg-world veteran, Josh Steiner ). In both instances, Bezos hoped to burnish the Post \xe2\x80\x99s reputation for strong journalism while also moving the paper away from its reputation as a liberal bulwark. Obviously, that has proven harder than he might have hoped.

Anyway, Bezos seems to have found another outlet for his frustrations in the endorsement decision. Indeed, if you\xe2\x80\x99re aspiring to create a nonpartisan newspaper\xe2\x80\x94and you\xe2\x80\x99re a little displeased with how that\xe2\x80\x99s been going\xe2\x80\x94it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t really make sense to compound reputational problems by once again telling people to vote for the Democratic presidential candidate, which is exactly what the Post has done since it began regularly endorsing candidates in 1976. Sure, even Bezos acknowledged that such a decision could and should have been made earlier, before the final stretch of this fraught and emotionally charged campaign, but such are the perils of being a titanic entrepreneur and business magnate with so many irons in the fire, and so many private European rail parties to attend.

Not surprisingly, the vast majority of the Post \xe2\x80\x99s own editorial board doesn\xe2\x80\x99t see it that way, and has said as much publicly. In a meeting this week, audio of which was obtained by The Washington Free Beacon, Shipley told his staff that he \xe2\x80\x9cmade very strenuous efforts, including a phone call [with Bezos] to change his mind, but failed.\xe2\x80\x9d Shipley\xe2\x80\x99s decision to create some daylight between himself and both Bezos and Lewis, who also wrote a mini-essay defending the non-endorsement decision, was either bold, dastardly, defensive, or a manifestation of naked survival instincts. Never the most popular guy in the building, Shipley seemingly recognized that\xe2\x80\x94in the short term, at least\xe2\x80\x94the eleventh-hour non-endorsement decision was going to deliver a far more severe reputational hit than one more cycle in which the never-Trumpers took solace in knowing the Post was firmly on their side. He also probably didn\xe2\x80\x99t want underlings to plot a coup against him, though Lewis couldn\xe2\x80\x99t be pleased that he broke rank.

In any event, there are at least two points that the critics of Bezos\xe2\x80\x99s decision may not fully appreciate. First, despite their worst fears, there is no evidence or indication that Bezos intends to tamper with the journalism itself. Any sober review of the Post \xe2\x80\x99s reporting in recent days, weeks, or months would find zero deviation from its long-standing effort to hold Trump to account. (Of course, great reporting has the potential to have far greater impact on a presidential election than any endorsement, which has exactly no bearing, but whatever.) The notion that Bezos intends to shy away from tough journalism to placate Trump is inconsistent with all the available evidence going back for nearly a decade.

Second, while this whole thing was grossly mismanaged, and has only compounded the newsroom\xe2\x80\x99s preexisting obsession with finding its leadership guilty of philosophical adultery, it\xe2\x80\x99s worth keeping in mind that the leaders have their own misgivings about the newsroom, too. Ultimately, Bezos wants to own, and Lewis wants to manage, a paper staffed by a team that supports their vision for semi-nonpartisan, future-proofed profitability. And while they certainly don\xe2\x80\x99t want, and didn\xe2\x80\x99t anticipate, such a severe exodus of subscribers, they\xe2\x80\x99re probably not terribly bothered by the departure of morally righteous journalists who pine for a return to the \xe2\x80\x9cDemocracy Dies in Darkness\xe2\x80\x9d era, no matter what Marty posts on Twitter. As is the case in any business transformation, they may even welcome the turnover.
FOUR STORIES WE\xe2\x80\x99RE TALKING ABOUT
Hollywood\xe2\x80\x99s Menendez Problem
Illuminating a fascinating political subplot in Hollywood.
ERIQ GARDNER
Fear & Polling in Las Vegas
Dissecting the early-voting surge with Nevada\xe2\x80\x99s election guru.
TARA PALMERI
An Art Market Mystery
On the Tom Wesselmann retrospective in Paris.
MARION MANEKER
The Puck Private Conversation
The quarterly results of our in-depth subscriber survey.
BARATUNDE THURSTON
